name: KAI Scheduler
tagline: A Kubernetes scheduler that allocates GPUs to AI workloads with fair-share and topology-aware policies
seoTitle: KAI Scheduler - GPU Scheduling for Kubernetes
metaDescription: KAI Scheduler is an open-source Kubernetes scheduler from NVIDIA that allocates GPUs to AI and ML workloads using fair-share, gang, and topology-aware policies.
overview: KAI Scheduler is a Kubernetes scheduler, originally from NVIDIA, that manages how GPU resources are allocated to AI and machine learning workloads. It runs as an add-on scheduler in your cluster, so you opt workloads into it per pod rather than replacing the default kube-scheduler.

It is built for clusters that mix many kinds of jobs at once: small interactive notebooks, large distributed training runs, and inference services, all competing for the same GPUs. It splits resources across queues and applies fairness rules so no single team starves the others, while still keeping the hardware busy.

As a compute orchestration tool, it sits between your workloads and the cluster's nodes. Platform teams use it to set quotas, priorities, and placement rules; developers just add a queue label and a scheduler name to their pods and let KAI decide where the work lands.
features: Gang (batch) scheduling: all pods in a group are placed together or not at all, which matters for distributed training
Fairness policies based on Dominant Resource Fairness (DRF), plus time-based fairshare that accounts for historical usage
Hierarchical queues with per-queue quotas, over-quota weights, limits, and priorities
Topology-Aware Scheduling (TAS) to place tightly coupled pods close together on the network
Bin-packing or spread placement, workload consolidation, and elastic scaling between min and max pod counts
Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) support for vendor GPUs via Kubernetes ResourceClaims; runs alongside other schedulers

gettingStarted.intro: KAI Scheduler installs into a Kubernetes cluster with Helm. You need a running cluster, the Helm CLI, and the NVIDIA GPU-Operator if you plan to schedule GPU workloads.

steps.heading: Install with Helm
steps.body: Install the scheduler from the published OCI chart into its own namespace. Replace <VERSION> with a release from the project's releases page.

steps.heading: Submit a pod to the scheduler
steps.body: To route a pod through KAI, set schedulerName to kai-scheduler and add the kai.scheduler/queue label. Default queues are created on install. Submit workloads from a dedicated namespace, not the kai-scheduler namespace.
steps.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: cpu-only-pod
  labels:
    kai.scheduler/queue: default-queue
spec:
  schedulerName: kai-scheduler
  containers:
    - name: main
      image: ubuntu
      args: ["sleep", "infinity"]
      resources:
        requests:
          cpu: 100m
          memory: 250M

steps.heading: Apply the pod
steps.body: Apply the manifest with kubectl and KAI will place it according to your queue and fairness policies.

useCases: Sharing a fleet of GPU nodes across several teams while enforcing per-team quotas and fair-share so no group monopolizes the cluster
Running distributed training jobs that need all their workers scheduled at once (gang scheduling) instead of partially placed
Mixing interactive notebooks, batch training, and inference on one cluster with priorities and preemption between them
Placing tightly coupled multi-GPU or multi-node workloads with topology awareness to keep network hops low
